---
title: "About"
toc: true
---

::: {.about-grid}

::: {.headshot}
Headshot
placeholder

(replace
images/headshot.jpg)
:::

<div>

I'm an analytics leader specializing in **media measurement** — TV attribution, lift
analysis, anomaly detection, and the reproducible reporting infrastructure that makes those
methodologies useful at scale.

I've spent the last decade building these frameworks at JuiceMedia, where I lead the
Analytics Insights function. My team measures campaigns across pharma, telemarketing,
and media verticals. Before that, I built the analytical foundations of my career in
financial services at Encore Capital Group.

What I care about is the connection between **rigorous measurement** and **operational
discipline**. Most analytics teams have one or the other. The teams that have both are the
teams whose work survives executive scrutiny — and whose analysts don't burn out building
the same deck every month.

</div>

:::

## How I think about analytics work

::: {.pullquote}
The job is not to produce decks. The job is to answer hard questions defensibly,
deliver the answers in formats stakeholders can actually use, and build the team and
infrastructure so neither of those things depends on heroics.

[The job description, as I understand it.]{.pullquote-attr}
:::

I optimize for three things, in this order:

**Methodological honesty.** If the data doesn't support the claim, the claim doesn't get
made. If the model has assumptions, the assumptions get stated. If we don't know, we say
we don't know. This is non-negotiable, even when stakeholders want certainty.

**Reproducible delivery.** Every recurring deliverable should be code-driven. I've written
[a methodology paper](posts/code-driven-reporting.html) on why. The short version: manual
report assembly is the largest source of avoidable cost and risk in modern analytics teams.

**Team capacity.** A senior analyst's time is the most valuable resource on any analytics
team. I structure work so my analysts spend their hours on novel questions, not on
reproducing answers we've already given.

## Career path

::: {.timeline}

::: {.tl-item}
[2020 – Present]{.tl-date}

#### Manager of Analytics Insights {.tl-title}
[JuiceMedia]{.tl-company}

- Lead a team of analysts measuring TV and multi-channel campaigns at DMA and national level
- Architect the probabilistic attribution and lift measurement frameworks the team uses
- Built and maintain the automated reporting infrastructure (Quarto, gt, AWS S3, Athena) that produces weekly client deliverables
- Define methodology standards and conduct technical reviews
- Partner with engineering, data science, and client success on cross-functional initiatives
:::

::: {.tl-item}
[2016 – 2020]{.tl-date}

#### Senior Analyst → Director {.tl-title}
[DirectAvenue / JuiceMedia]{.tl-company}

- Promoted from Senior Analyst through Manager to Director, building the company's
  measurement practice from the ground up
- Designed the spike detection framework now used across pharma and telemarketing campaigns
- Owned the methodology for DMA-level lift analysis used in monthly executive reviews
:::

::: {.tl-item}
[2012 – 2016]{.tl-date}

#### Data Analyst → Senior Analyst {.tl-title}
[Encore Capital Group]{.tl-company}

- Developed analytical reporting and statistical models for financial services operations
- Learned the discipline of communicating data-driven findings to non-technical stakeholders
- Built the foundational SQL and analytical skills that everything since has built on
:::

::: {.tl-item}
[2008 – 2012]{.tl-date}

#### B.A. Economics & Statistics {.tl-title}
[San Diego State University]{.tl-company}

- Double major: Economics (Quantitative Analysis concentration) and Statistics
- Combined economic theory with applied statistical methods
:::

:::

## Skills, organized honestly

The list of tools I'm comfortable with would be long and not particularly useful. Here's
what I'm actually known for:

**Causal measurement** — TV attribution, lift analysis, synthetic controls,
probabilistic attribution. The methodology side of media measurement.

**Anomaly detection** — Statistical and ML-based approaches for spike detection,
applied to web traffic, conversions, and operational metrics.

**Reproducible reporting** — Quarto, R Markdown, parameterized rendering, gt tables,
publication-quality output. The engineering side of analytics work.

**Data foundation** — SQL, Parquet, partitioning strategy, AWS (S3, Athena), Polars,
DuckDB. The storage and query layer that makes everything else fast.

**Stakeholder communication** — Translating between Bayesian intervals and "yes, this
worked" without losing information in either direction.

**Team leadership** — Managing analysts (3+ direct reports), defining methodology
standards, conducting technical reviews, mentoring junior team members through harder
problems.

## Get in touch

I'm open to conversations about **Manager and Director-level analytics roles** —
particularly ones where the methodology and the engineering both matter, and the team has
real questions worth answering.

📧 [idgesus@gmail.com](mailto:idgesus@gmail.com)
💼 [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/in/YOURUSERNAME)
🔗 [GitHub](https://github.com/YOURUSERNAME)
📄 [Resume](resume.html)
